title: Design Netflix for kids
clarifying_qs: Is this a new Netflix vertical or a standalone product like YouTube Kids?
Only entertainment, or learning/educational content as well?
Who creates the content - Netflix or users? How do we control quality?
Why this over YouTube Kids?
Is it subscription-based?
What age group of kids are we targeting?
Geography: world or India?
user_segments: Parents - aware, set up and control the account
pain_points: Parents worry about content safety and age-appropriateness
Hard to control quality of kid-targeted content
Kids need age-segmented discovery, not generic catalogs
Parents need easy setup and parental controls
framework_steps: Clarify scope and goal
Identify user/customer segments (kids, siblings, parents, teachers)
Surface unmet needs and pain points
Prioritize a segment
Brainstorm solutions
Define success metrics

sections.heading: Clarifying Questions
bullets: New vertical vs. standalone product (Netflix-for-kids like YouTube Kids)
Entertainment only or studies as well
Content supply: who creates it and how is quality controlled
Why not YouTube; subscription model; age group; geo (world vs. India)

sections.heading: User / Customer Segments
bullets: Kids (segmented by age) - the customer
Siblings - both customer and user
Parents - the aware setup/control user
Teachers - aware users who recommend

title: How would you design parking features on Google Maps?
clarifying_qs: Same consumer Google Maps we use to find addresses and places?
New parking feature vs. enhancement of the existing one?
What's the goal - engagement, monetization, or experience?
Which vehicle types - cars only, no bikes/trucks?
Geography - US market?
Are we monetizing at launch?
user_segments: Supply: commercial properties with marked parking (offices, malls, stadiums, restaurants)
Demand - people going out for fun/errands (non-work)
Demand - tourists/infrequent visitors; offices booking spaces for employees
pain_points: Regular commuters waste time driving through garages hunting for a spot
Casual visitors don't know if parking exists near a destination
No real-time capacity / availability information
Property owners rely on manual processes; underutilized inventory
Roadside/street parking availability is invisible
framework_steps: Clarify problem, scope and goal (engagement, US, cars, no monetization)
Segment users into supply and demand
Identify pain points per segment
Prioritize segment and pain points
Brainstorm and prioritize solutions (impact x effort)
Define success metrics and tradeoffs

sections.heading: Problem Definition & Scope
bullets: Design parking features for Google Maps in the US market
Cars only; no monetization at initial launch
Two-sided marketplace: supply (commercial properties) and demand (drivers)

sections.heading: User Segmentation
bullets: Supply: offices, malls, restaurants, large venues/stadiums with dedicated parking
Demand: regular commuters, fun/entertainment outings, errand runners
Offices booking spaces for their employees

sections.heading: Pain Points
bullets: Users: uncertainty about availability; time wasted driving through garages; no real-time info
Property owners: manual payment/management; underutilized inventory; no digital integration

sections.heading: Solution & Prioritization
bullets: High impact / low effort: 'parking available' flag and capacity count on business profiles
Live capacity tracking with color-coded indicators (blue/orange/red) like traffic
Human-assisted updates for street parking; Street View analysis as a moonshot
Demand-side: book a slot in advance for same day

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: North Star: views/clicks on parking info in business profiles (availability, slots)
Engagement: parking feature usage vs. total navigation sessions; time-to-park

sections.heading: Tradeoffs
bullets: Data accuracy is critical - wrong availability info badly hurts UX
Supply-side adoption is hard without immediate monetization

title: Design a recommendation system experience for an e-commerce app
clarifying_qs: Are we designing the UX and inputs/outputs, not the ML model?
Web, mobile, or both?
Everything-store (Amazon/Flipkart), niche store, or quick-commerce?
What input criteria do we have to start?
Which real estate - homepage, search page, or product listing?
user_segments: New users still learning the UX
Frequent shoppers wanting better recommendations and 'pick up where you left off'
pain_points: New users don't understand the recommendation UX
Frequent shoppers get generic, not personalized, recommendations
Return shoppers can't easily resume prior browsing intent
Monthly shoppers want category-based essentials curation, not exploration
framework_steps: Clarify scope (UX not ML, everything-store, both platforms)
Map the user journey (download, open, land on home screen)
Segment users by frequency and demographic
Identify pain points
Propose curated solutions per segment
Define product vision and metrics

sections.heading: Clarify & Problem
bullets: Focus is recommendation UX + inputs/outputs, not the ML model
Everything-store like Amazon/Flipkart, web and mobile
Real estate: homepage and search page (product listing is P2)

sections.heading: User Segments & Pain Points
bullets: New users: understanding the UX
Frequent shoppers: better recommendations, resume where they left off
Monthly/transactional shoppers: essentials curation

sections.heading: Solution
bullets: Robust home screen with simple products tuned to the demographic
Return shoppers: curated list of products viewed, ranked by reviews/price
Monthly shoppers: curate by category (kitchen essentials, groceries, living room, furniture)

sections.heading: Product Vision
bullets: Better recommendations for shoppers who frequently shop

title: Design a system to track reviews abuse on Amazon.com
clarifying_qs: What is meant by abuse - fake/paid reviews?
Any particular country, or global?
Have we seen an increase in fake reviews?
Can we quantify the % of fake reviews and the recent increase?
user_segments: Reviewers who did not buy the product
Reviewers who bought but did not use the product
Reviewers posting for compensation/reward from the seller
Reviewers posting negative reviews to malign a seller (possibly colluding with competitors)
pain_points: Prospective buyers can't get accurate feedback from real buyers
Data corruption erodes Amazon's platform value
Direct impact on customer centricity and revenue
Sellers unfairly maligned by colluding competitors
framework_steps: Anchor to Amazon's vision (most customer-centric company)
Define review abuse precisely
Identify the abuser personas/segments
Design a detection + mitigation solution per persona
Run experiments and measure delta on quality metrics

sections.heading: Defining Review Abuse
bullets: Reviews from people who didn't buy, bought-but-didn't-use, posted for reward, or posted to malign a seller
Dangerous because it corrupts data, hurts buyers, and damages customer centricity and revenue

sections.heading: Solutions by Persona
bullets: Not bought: if variance vs. verified buyers is large, restrict non-verified reviews or use AI to detect/shadow-ban fakes
Bought-not-used: detect immediate post-delivery positive reviews and lower their rating contribution
Paid/incentivized: flag high-frequency positive reviewers; assign a 0-1 'fakeness score' and exclude high scores
Malicious negative: detect patterns; restrict to verified buyers or require photo/video context

sections.heading: Experiments & Metrics
bullets: Run experiments to find the best delta improvement before scaling
Avg fakeness score pre/post; % of customers finding reviews helpful
% verified vs. non-verified buyer reviews; ARPU (long-horizon)

title: Design a feature for Zepto to reduce cart abandonment caused by out-of-stock items at checkout
clarifying_qs: Which user segment - repeat buyers vs. new users?
Which product category is most affected?
Why do OOS items cause abandonment vs. substitution?
Who decides the substitute - user or algorithm?
What's the trust mechanism for substitutions?
user_segments: Value buyers who want control (pick their own)
New users sensitive to first-experience trust
pain_points: An OOS item at checkout breaks the basket and the user abandons
Users don't trust algorithmic substitutions
10-min delivery promise means substitution must not break the SLA
No pre-cart signal that an item is running low
framework_steps: Clarify which segment and category
Probe the problem depth (why OOS causes abandonment)
Explore solution space (pre-cart signals, real-time alternatives, save-for-later, preference-based substitution)
Prioritise on user impact x feasibility x business value
Define MVP, sketch the flow, state constraints

sections.heading: Strong Answer Hints
bullets: Anchor 'show alternatives': who decides (user vs. algorithm) and what's the trust mechanism
ICP split: convenience buyer wants auto-substitute; value buyer wants to pick their own
Zepto-specific: substitution must not break the 10-minute SLA

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: cart-to-order conversion for sessions with 1+ OOS item
Counter-metric: substitution rejection rate

sections.heading: Activation Angle
bullets: Launch opt-in 'Smart Replace' preference at onboarding - users who set it convert ~2x better on OOS events

title: Design a feature for Zepto to reduce cart abandonment among users who add items but never check out
clarifying_qs: Which segment - new vs. repeat, high-AOV vs. impulse buyers?
Why do they abandon - price shock, distraction, no urgency, payment friction?
Which persona do we go deep on?
What's the cold-chain constraint on holding perishables?
What's the entry point and trigger timing?
user_segments: Busy urban millennials doing a 10-minute grocery run
High-intent returners with multiple abandoned carts
pain_points: Price shock at the cart leads to drop-off
Distractions interrupt the checkout
No urgency to complete the order
Payment friction at the final step
Perishables can't be held indefinitely (cold-chain constraint)
framework_steps: Clarify scope and segment
Identify why they abandon
Define one persona and go deep
Ideate 3-4 features, rank by impact vs. effort
Pick one and spec the UX, entry point, edge cases
Define metrics and tradeoffs

sections.heading: Strong Answer Hints
bullets: Don't just 'send a push' - anchor to why they abandoned (price? forgot? browsing?)
Segment: cart >Rs.800 not checked out in 15 min -> 'slot expiry nudge' with 1-tap checkout
Cold-chain constraint is a moat - Zepto can't hold perishables, so urgency messaging is product-native

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: cart-to-checkout conversion rate (by segment)
Counter-metric: push notification opt-outs / notification fatigue score

sections.heading: Activation Angle
bullets: Trigger only after 2nd abandoned cart in 7 days - re-engages high-intent returners without annoying new users

title: Design a feature for Meesho to help first-time buyers in Tier 3 cities who browse but never complete their first purchase
clarifying_qs: Who exactly is the user - Tier 3, first-time, price-sensitive?
Which platform - the app?
What does 'complete first purchase' mean?
Why do they browse but not buy - trust, payment anxiety, peer validation, price doubt?
Any constraint against a drastic UI overhaul?
user_segments: Users with a trust deficit about delivery and product quality
pain_points: Trust deficit - unsure if the product will actually arrive
Payment anxiety - reluctance to prepay
No peer/social validation for the purchase
Price doubt and language barriers
Final checkout step feels risky
framework_steps: Clarify scope, user and 'complete first purchase' definition
Understand why they browse but don't buy
Define goals and constraints (no drastic UI overhaul)
Ideate solutions ranked by impact/effort
Pick 1-2 and spec flows and edge cases
Define success metrics

sections.heading: Strong Answer Hints
bullets: Go deeper than 'add a discount' - address Tier 3 first-buyer psychology (trust deficit, delivery doubt, language)
Build trust signals: COD prominence, delivery promise, social proof from nearby pincodes
Tie back to Meesho's identity: reseller-driven, WhatsApp-heavy social commerce

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: first-purchase conversion (browse -> order placed) within 7 days of install
Counter-metric: don't tank AOV or raise returns by over-incentivising low-trust buys

sections.heading: Activation Angle
bullets: 'Your first order, guaranteed' trust widget on the PDP for new users
Shows pincode delivery estimate, COD badge, and one real buyer review from a nearby city

title: What is your favorite product and why? (Duolingo)
clarifying_qs: Which product would you pick and why?
What core problems does it solve?
What makes it unique vs. alternatives?
How does it tie to the company's broader vision?
user_segments: Learners who find traditional learning boring
Time-constrained learners who need short sessions
pain_points: Traditional language classes are expensive with fixed schedules
Classroom-style learning is repetitive and boring
Learning requires large 1-hour, high-effort blocks
Hard to stay consistent and build a habit
framework_steps: Pick the product and state the high-level why
Accessibility: free/low-cost, always available
Engagement: gamified streaks, rewards, dopamine loops
Time/consistency: bite-sized 5-10 min sessions
Wrap as a habit-building product and tie to vision

sections.heading: Why Duolingo Works (3 levers)
bullets: Accessibility: removes cost/access barriers - free or low-cost, always available
Engagement: gamification, streaks, rewards turn boring into addictive
Time: bite-sized 5-10 min sessions fit into your day and build a habit

sections.heading: PM Wrap
bullets: It's not just a learning product, it's a habit-building product
Reduces cost, reduces effort, increases engagement - which is why it works at scale

sections.heading: Vision & Uniqueness
bullets: Ties to making education accessible globally (math, music, chess expansions)
Killer line: many products teach language, but Duolingo optimizes for daily habit formation at scale

title: App Critique: Critique LinkedIn's job application flow - identify the top 3 friction points and how you'd fix each
clarifying_qs: Who's the user - active job seeker, passive browser, or recruiter?
Which flow - search > job page > Easy Apply or external redirect > status tracking?
Where do users drop off or get confused?
How do we prioritize the friction points?
Should fixes tie back to LinkedIn's recruiter revenue model?
pain_points: Easy Apply black hole - apply and hear nothing, no status transparency
Resume re-entry hell - manual form fill on external redirects despite an existing profile
Job relevance mismatch - off-target recommendations with no feedback loop
Anxiety and churn from lack of application status
framework_steps: Set context - who's the user
Walk the flow - search to apply to status tracking
Identify friction at drop-off and confusion points
Prioritise top 3 by frequency x severity
Propose specific fixes, each tied to a beneficiary and a metric

sections.heading: Top 3 Friction Points
bullets: Easy Apply black hole - no status transparency creates anxiety and platform churn
Resume re-entry hell - manual field fill on external redirects despite a full profile
Job relevance mismatch - off-target recommendations, no quick feedback to teach the algo

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: application completion rate (Easy Apply started -> submitted)
Counter-metric: recruiter response rate on submitted applications (quality, not just volume)

sections.heading: Activation Angle
bullets: Launch an 'Application Status Hub' tab (like Uber trip history) - status visibility reduces churn from job-seeking mode

title: Critique the onboarding flow of LinkedIn for a fresh college graduate looking for their first job - what's broken, what works, and what are your top 3 fixes?
clarifying_qs: Who's the user - fresh grad, 21-23, limited experience, anxious about an empty profile?
What's the current flow - sign up > profile fill > connections > feed > job search?
Where does a fresh grad feel stuck or embarrassed?
What should the flow do for this specific user vs. the experienced default?
How do fixes tie to LinkedIn's engagement + placement + premium goals?
user_segments: Fresh grads (21-23) with no job title or recommendations
Tier 2/3 college students excluded by IIT/IIM-first networks
Students with projects/coursework but no work history
pain_points: Profile-completeness/strength bar penalizes things grads can't control - creates anxiety not momentum
Connection recommendations are too broad - strangers, not classmates or professors
First feed is senior-exec thought leadership, irrelevant to a new grad's job search
No designed 'first win' moment - user leaves without feeling progress
Aggressive early profile-photo prompt adds friction before value
framework_steps: Set user context - new grad with empty profile
Walk the flow - sign up to job search
Identify 3-5 friction points with severity (High/Medium/Low)
Propose specific, buildable fixes with the 'why'
Tie to LinkedIn's goal - engagement + placement -> premium

sections.heading: Key Friction Points
bullets: [High] Front-loaded completeness score makes empty profiles feel shameful (imposter syndrome)
[High] Connection recommendations are random strangers, not classmates/professors
[Medium] First feed is senior-exec content, not relevant to job search
[Medium] No designed 'first win' moment; [Low] aggressive early photo prompt

sections.heading: Top 3 Fixes
bullets: Progressive profile completion - unlock sections as the user engages ('add experience next?')
Cohort-based connection suggestions - surface classmates, alumni, professors via education data; offer a 'Student Mode' (projects/coursework/skills)
Design a 'first win' - celebrate first accepted connection + show a relevant job listing in the same flow

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: % of new grads who complete a profile AND apply to >=1 job within 7 days (or reach All-Star in 7 days)
Counter-metric: don't over-optimize completeness; track Day 3 retention and application-to-response rate

sections.heading: Activation Angle
bullets: 'First Job Readiness' Day-1 checklist (add a skill, follow 3 companies, connect with 5 classmates)
Activation event: first connection accepted + a job listing viewed in the same session

title: Create a 1-year strategy (Now/Next/Later) - Swiggy International Expansion
clarifying_qs: What does winning look like at month 12 (the North Star)?
Are we picking a specific market or going global?
City-first or country-first?
What constraints does Swiggy's current state impose?
How should each phase have its own success metric?
user_segments: Indian diaspora in Dubai (largest warm audience)
pain_points: Swiggy has zero brand with non-Indian consumers and no supply outside India
Logistics fleet must be rebuilt from scratch abroad
Incumbents (Talabat, Uber Eats, Deliveroo) have multi-year head starts
Talabat is dominant but has service-quality gaps Swiggy can exploit
framework_steps: Set the North Star (what winning looks like at month 12)
Diagnose current state (assets and liabilities)
Pick the entry market - city-first, defended (Dubai)
Now (0-3m): Foundation - supply before demand
Next (3-6m): Demand engine - first 100K users
Later (6-12m): Defensibility - subscription + lock-in
Set phase-level metrics

sections.heading: North Star
bullets: #1 food delivery app by order volume in one target city by month 12
D30 retention >= 25%; contribution margin positive by month 10

sections.heading: Diagnose & Entry Market
bullets: Assets: supply-side ops, hyperlocal/10-min know-how, diaspora brand, mature tech
Liabilities: no non-Indian brand, zero supply/fleet abroad, strong incumbents
Entry market: Dubai - 1M+ Indians, Talabat dominant but not loved, high AOV, English-friendly
City-within-city: start with Dubai Marina, Downtown, Bur Dubai

sections.heading: Now (0-3 months): Foundation
bullets: Rule: no consumer marketing until supply exists
Target 200 restaurant partners before consumer launch (Indian supply first + top non-Indian)
12% commission land grab vs. Talabat's 18-22%; 20 exclusive anchor restaurants
Hire 50 delivery partners; localize app (AED, Arabic, local payments)

sections.heading: Next (3-6 months): Demand Engine
bullets: Target 100K users with D7 repeat >= 30%
Diaspora-first marketing via community influencers; launch on Diwali/Eid/cricket day
Referral program (coefficient 0.4), corporate accounts (TCS/Infosys/Wipro), residential complex partnerships

sections.heading: Later (6-12 months): Defensibility
bullets: Swiggy One Dubai subscription (target 20% MAU attach)
Instamart Dubai dark stores (Indian grocery gap Talabat is weak on)
Restaurant loyalty/Swiggy Select lock-in; evaluate city #2 (Abu Dhabi vs. Singapore)

sections.heading: Metrics by Phase
bullets: Now: 200 restaurant partners before launch (counter: restaurant churn, beta app rating)
Next: 100K users, D7 repeat >= 30% (counter: restaurant fill rate, unit economics)
Later: D30 >= 25%, CM positive by M10, Swiggy One attach >= 20%

title: WhatsApp monetization strategy - no ads, no extension of current offerings (no business messaging, UPI, or stores)
clarifying_qs: Is there a geography constraint? (India vs. Brazil vs. Europe differ)
Is the goal net-new revenue streams or converting free users to paying?
Can we monetize identity, community, or creator reach without ads?
What does WhatsApp actually own that's valuable and undermonetized?
What time horizon and risk tolerance to the core experience?
user_segments: Power users - entrepreneurs, freelancers, small business owners (60M+ in India)
B2B companies needing identity verification (fintech, healthcare, gig)
pain_points: WhatsApp's most valuable assets (identity, trust, community, reach) are undermonetized
Constraints rule out ads and all current offerings
Power users lack premium features (multi-device, scheduling, editing)
Creators already hack monetization manually via UPI
framework_steps: Clarify constraints and assumptions (4-beat open)
Reframe: what does WhatsApp actually own that's valuable?
Generate genuine new levers (Premium, Identity API, Communities, Status)
Go deep on two; pick one bet and defend it
Metrics and counter-metrics

sections.heading: The Reframe
bullets: Constraints force first principles: monetize identity, community trust, and creator reach
Assets: 1B+ DAU, trusted channel, verified identity, relationship graph, Status, Groups

sections.heading: Four New Levers
bullets: WhatsApp Premium (consumer subscription) - multi-device, scheduling, editing, custom domains; Rs.99/$2.99 mo
Identity Infrastructure API - pay-per-verification for KYC/healthcare/gig (not messaging)
Communities monetization - paid groups, content subscriptions, ticketed events; 15-20% take rate
Status creator economy - subscriptions, digital-goods commerce, tipping, analytics

sections.heading: The One Bet
bullets: WhatsApp Premium - lowest risk to core, fastest to build, proven by Telegram Premium
Even 0.5% of 1B DAU at $2/mo = ~$120M ARR; sequence Premium -> Communities -> Status -> Identity

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: MRR from new streams; Supporting: paid conversion, creator transaction volume, verification volume
Counter: core DAU retention, message volume per user, Premium churn

title: How would you grow UberEats' user base?
clarifying_qs: Which market and timeframe? Any constraints?
Are we focused on net-new acquisition or reactivation?
Who are the distinct user segments?
Why are they not using / churning / not converting?
Any budget or channel constraints?
user_segments: Tried once and dropped (bad first experience)
pain_points: Tier 2 price gap and local-restaurant supply gap
Competitor users have no reason to switch (loyalty discounts)
Never-tried users have awareness and trust gaps
Occasional users haven't formed a habit
framework_steps: Clarify scope (market, timeframe, constraints)
Segment the user base
Diagnose why they're not converting/churning
Identify growth levers (supply, demand, habit, switching)
Pick ONE bet and defend it
Metrics + counter-metrics and an activation plan

sections.heading: Segmentation & Diagnosis
bullets: Focus on never-tried (Tier 2) + competitor switchers - highest TAM headroom
Tier 2: price + supply gap; competitor users: habit + switching cost; never-tried: awareness + trust

sections.heading: Growth Levers
bullets: Supply expansion in Tier 2 with subsidised first-year fees and city-indexed cuisines
First-order economics: loss-leader discounting, Uber ride-credit bundles
Switching: port ratings, match Swiggy One with Uber One, speed guarantee
Virality: group ordering, corporate ordering

sections.heading: The Bet
bullets: Uber One cross-product bundling (rides + food) - a structural moat Swiggy/Zomato cannot replicate

sections.heading: Metrics & Activation
bullets: Primary: new user activations/week by segment+city; Supporting: D7 repeat, Uber One attach
Counter: CAC / first-order contribution margin
Activation: Uber One trial surfaced on ride confirmation, Day 1 first food order, Day 7 savings nudge

title: How would you 10x Duolingo?
clarifying_qs: What does 10x mean - engagement, revenue, or acquisition?
Global or a particular market like the USA?
Any constraints or timeline (e.g. 5 years for revenue, 1 year for MAU)?
Duolingo as a standalone product or part of a larger company?
Any limits on the ideas/products we can launch?
user_segments: People of school age (highest priority - size + brain malleability)
pain_points: Low free-to-paid conversion caps revenue
Duolingo monetizes learning time, not the economic outcome (jobs, admission, immigration)
Uneven/poor language instruction worldwide for school-age learners
Incumbent credentials (TOEFL/IELTS) are expensive, slow, offline
framework_steps: Clarify what 10x means (revenue or MAU)
Diagnose the current growth engine (acquisition, engagement, monetization)
Find the strategic gap (outcome vs. learning time)
Bet: become the global language credential platform
Add revenue multipliers (hiring, immigration, enterprise)
Name risks and success metrics

sections.heading: Revenue Levers (Conventional)
bullets: Number of users: emerging markets, SEO/SEM/AEO, influencer tie-ups, affiliate + edtech partnerships, M&A
Free-to-paid: smaller packs, reactivation, personalized report cards, AI-tutor free preview
ARPU: light ads for paying users, in-progress ad experiments, buy small packs of hearts/lives

sections.heading: Strategic Bet: Credential Platform
bullets: Reposition from 'language learning app' to 'global standard for proving language ability'
Continuous skill certification + live language profile; global credential network with universities/employers/immigration
Paid verification, proctoring, enterprise validation (free learning, $50-100 certification)

sections.heading: Revenue Multipliers
bullets: Hiring marketplace filtering by Duolingo scores
Immigration services (visa/residency language proof)
Professional upskilling / enterprise B2B

sections.heading: Risks & Metrics
bullets: Risks: institutional credibility, cheating/fraud, slow adoption, product complexity
Primary: verified certifications issued, certification revenue, institutional partnerships

title: How would you position this product vs. alternatives? (Notion vs. Confluence + Google Docs)
clarifying_qs: What category are we actually playing in (or creating)?
Who is the primary buyer, and who is Notion NOT for?
What do customers actually switch from?
Where do the alternatives structurally fail?
How do we validate that positioning is landing in-product?
user_segments: Ops/eng lead or founding team at a 20-500 person startup drowning in Google Docs
ICs who can't find anything in the company wiki
Teams that have outgrown Docs but aren't ready for Confluence
NOT: 10k-person enterprises, quick-note phone users, deep Jira-style trackers
pain_points: Confluence: rigid hierarchy, terrible editor - written in but never read
Google Docs: completely flat - no structure, linking, or database layer
Neither offers connected, structured flexibility
Notion's own steeper learning curve and large-wiki performance
framework_steps: Define the category (connected workspace)
Identify the target customer - and who it's NOT for
Name the real alternatives and their structural failures
Find the ownable, defensible gap
Craft the positioning statement
Proof points and the aha moment
Metrics to validate positioning is landing

sections.heading: Category & Target
bullets: Category: 'connected workspace' (not a notes app or wiki - both shrink the market and price point)
Buyer: ops/eng lead at a 20-500 person startup; explicitly NOT enterprises, phone-note users, or Jira-style teams

sections.heading: The Gap
bullets: Confluence gives structure but no flexibility; Google Docs gives flexibility but no structure
Neither gives connected, structured flexibility - that gap is Notion's territory

sections.heading: Positioning Statement
bullets: 'The connected workspace for teams who've outgrown Google Docs but aren't ready to get trapped in Confluence'
One place where docs, projects, and knowledge live together - built the way your team thinks

sections.heading: Proof Points & Aha Moment
bullets: Bi-directional linking, database relations, block-based editor, AI built in, templates marketplace
Aha moment: the first time a user links a doc to a database (within first 10-15 min) - engineer onboarding toward it

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: trial-to-paid conversion by acquisition source; 'what were you using before?' at signup
Supporting: % connecting a database to a doc in first session; Counter: time-to-first-value, 30-day churn reason

title: Monetization: How would you price/package Uber Mobility?
clarifying_qs: Who is actually paying - rider, driver, enterprise, advertiser?
What value are they getting (certainty, speed, predictability, comfort)?
What is each segment willing to pay?
What model fits - per-use, subscription, weekly pass, enterprise contract?
How do we activate a new pricing tier, not just announce it?
user_segments: Daily commuters (predictable, surge-sensitive, high monthly spend)
Occasional users (transactional, will pay for convenience)
Premium users (low price sensitivity, want comfort/reliability)
Corporate travelers and price-sensitive students/Tier 2 riders
pain_points: Surge is a trust killer for daily commuters who then switch to Metro/autos
Uber One is too broad - too pricey for students, too generic for executives
No frequency reward for light users
Driver income is unpredictable, feeding back into surge
framework_steps: Who is paying (segment the payer)
What value are they getting
What are they willing to pay (segment WTP)
What model fits
How to package (tiers, bundles, upsell)
Metrics + activation plan + supply-side

sections.heading: Pricing Architecture
bullets: Tier 1: Pay-per-ride (keep surge - margin lives here) for occasional users
Tier 2 (THE BET): Uber Pass Rs.49/week, 10 rides, no surge - for daily commuters
Tier 3: Uber One Rs.149/mo refined to protect surge on all ride types
Tier 4: Uber Business (enterprise); Tier 5: Uber Premier Pass Rs.799/mo for executives

sections.heading: Why Uber Pass (Weekly)
bullets: Rs.49 is an impulse buy; weekly reset creates a Monday re-engagement moment
Frame: 'Rs.4.90 per ride guaranteed, no surge ever' - wins against Metro
Lower commitment = faster trial = faster habit, then upsell to monthly

sections.heading: Activation & Supply Side
bullets: Target 3+ weekday rides; trigger Monday 8am: 'Lock in 10 rides for Rs.49, no surge'
Day 1 'pass active', Day 7 'you saved Rs.X in surge'
Pass rides get a small driver income top-up so supply stays reliable

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: subscription attach rate; Supporting: Pass weekly renewal, rides/week Pass vs. non-Pass
Counter: blended take-rate per ride, driver acceptance on Pass rides

title: Monetization: How would you price/package? (Uber Mobility + Swiggy Food Delivery)
clarifying_qs: Who pays - user, driver, restaurant, advertiser?
What value are they getting?
What is each segment willing to pay?
What model fits - per-use, subscription, freemium, marketplace take-rate?
How do you package into tiers and bundles, and how do you activate them?
user_segments: Uber: occasional riders, frequent commuters, corporate accounts, daily commuters
Swiggy: casual users, students/low-frequency, power users, families/high-AOV
pain_points: One subscription doesn't fit all (students want cheap, professionals want speed, families want value)
Occasional riders pay full surge while frequent riders need predictability
Drivers need income stability
Restaurant commissions can be squeezed when funding subscriptions
framework_steps: Segment the payer (user, driver, restaurant, advertiser)
Identify value delivered per segment
Assess willingness to pay
Choose a model that fits
Package into tiers and bundles
Pick one bet, add activation and metrics

sections.heading: Uber Mobility Tiers
bullets: Pay-per-ride (occasional), Uber One Rs.149/mo (frequent), Uber Business (corporate)
The bet: Uber Pass Rs.49/week for 10 rides - daily commuters, weekly = low commitment, fast habit

sections.heading: Swiggy Food Delivery Tiers
bullets: Free (casual), Swiggy One Lite Rs.99/mo (students/low-freq), Swiggy One Rs.299/mo (power), Swiggy Max Rs.499/mo (families)
The bet: Swiggy One Lite Rs.99 - untapped segment who want a sub but can't justify Rs.299; drives free->paid

sections.heading: Activation
bullets: Trigger after 3rd order/ride; Day 1 welcome + first free benefit; Day 7 savings summary

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: subscription attach rate; Supporting: ARPU by tier, Lite->One upgrade rate within 90 days
Counter: restaurant commission blended rate (don't compress margins funding subs)

title: A competitor launched the same feature cheaper. What do you do? (Spotify + Instagram)
clarifying_qs: Is this actually a threat, and how big - to which segment?
Where are we structurally vulnerable vs. structurally strong?
Should we match, differentiate, or ignore?
What's the one focused response we'll commit to?
What metric tells us the response is working?
user_segments: Spotify: audiophiles (~5%) vs. the mass earbud/laptop-speaker majority
Spotify premium subscribers at churn risk
Instagram: top 1% creators driving ~80% of Reels volume
pain_points: Spotify weak on audio quality perception and hardware integration (Apple owns AirPods)
Instagram creator monetization is opaque; algorithm is a black box
Risk of overreacting and eating margin across 100% of users for a 5% threat
Creator flywheel can spin in reverse if top creators leave
framework_steps: Reality check - is this a threat, how big, to whom
Analyze the gap - where vulnerable vs. strong
Decide response - match / differentiate / ignore
Act on one bet
Results + recheck with a tied metric

sections.heading: Spotify - Apple Music Free Spatial Audio
bullets: Reality: affects ~5% audiophiles; threat medium but contained - don't drop price
Spotify's moat is social + discovery + multi-format + cross-platform, NOT audio quality
Act: Spotify Jam 2.0 (social listening), AI DJ expansion, Spotify HiFi paid tier

sections.heading: Instagram - TikTok Longer Video + Better Creator Monetization
bullets: Reality: HIGH, potentially existential for the Reels creator flywheel
Own what TikTok can't: brand-to-creator commerce ('TikTok = go viral, Instagram = get paid')
Act: Creator Marketplace 2.0, monetization transparency dashboard, longer Reels (table stakes), brand 'Collab' co-posts

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Spotify: premium retention rate, churn-reason survey, Jam/HiFi adoption (counter: new-subscriber CAC)
Instagram: top-10K creator retention, brand-deal volume via Marketplace (counter: Reels watch time)

sections.heading: What NOT to Do
bullets: Don't match the price/feature, don't run 'we're just as good' campaigns - signals insecurity

title: Should we enter a new market/vertical? How do you decide? (Apple for AI/LLMs)
clarifying_qs: Does entering align with where the company is strategically going?
Is the market big enough, and are we early or late?
Do we have a right to win - what's the moat?
What's the mode of entry - build, buy, partner, or invest?
How will we know it's working?
user_segments: Developers building on Apple AI frameworks
Users frustrated with Siri vs. modern LLMs
pain_points: Siri is embarrassing vs. GPT-4; AI moving to the cloud threatens Apple's experience control
Apple is late to foundation models with no public model or data moat
Window is closing as Android locks in on-device Gemini
Privacy expectations must be preserved
framework_steps: Strategic fit - does this align with where we're going
TAM + Timing - is the market big, are we early/late
Advantage - do we have a right to win
Mode of entry - build, buy, partner, invest
Proof + Metrics

sections.heading: STAMP Analysis
bullets: Strategic fit: entering AI is defensive; on-device, privacy-first AI is the next hardware moat
TAM/Timing: $100B+ by 2030; late to models but early to on-device privacy-preserving AI
Advantage: can't win the LLM race, but can win the interface layer via distribution + Apple Silicon + privacy
Mode: don't build foundation models; build the experience layer, partner for the model (OpenAI), acquire talent

sections.heading: Verdict
bullets: Enter - but as the AI experience/OS layer, not a model company
Moat is privacy + hardware + distribution, not the model

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: weekly Siri active query volume; Supporting: Apple Intelligence adoption, developer API calls
Counter: privacy incident reports / App Store trust score

title: You're a PM at Meta. Why should Meta continue investing in Reels? What goals and metrics would you set?
clarifying_qs: Meta Reels or Instagram Reels?
What was the initial objective, time since launch, and current standing?
What is Meta's North Star (MAU)?
Does 'investing' mean maintain current effort or develop new features?
Which platform synergy matters (Facebook + Instagram)?
user_segments: Creators / content producers (influencers, brands, everyday users)
pain_points: TikTok and YouTube Shorts pull younger users and creator attention
Risk of losing engagement leadership in short-form video
Short-form monetization is still evolving
Bad recommendations or low-quality content cause drop-off
framework: Mission Alignment -> Why Invest -> Goals -> Metrics
framework_steps: Clarify (Meta vs IG Reels, history, North Star)
Align Reels to Meta's mission
Justify continued investment (competition, engagement, ads, creators, synergy)
Set goals (engagement, retention, growth)
Define viewer + creator metrics with guardrails

sections.heading: Why Continue Investing
bullets: Competitive defense vs. TikTok/YouTube Shorts to protect engagement leadership
Engagement: short-form keeps users longer; Ad revenue: new in-feed/branded formats
Creator ecosystem and cross-platform synergy with Facebook/Instagram

sections.heading: Goals
bullets: Maximize engagement, creator retention, and monetization as a still-maturing product

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: North Star: average watch time per user
Viewer: reels viewed/shared per day, weekly retention; Creator: uploads/creator, viewer->creator conversion
Business: Reels ad revenue growth (ARPU)

sections.heading: Guardrails
bullets: Reels churn/drop-off rate, top-creator retention, ad-load perception, % of total IG time on Reels

title: Estimate the total number of ride-hailing trips completed in Bangalore on a typical Monday
clarifying_qs: Trips completed vs. trips requested?
All ride-hailing or one provider?
Include autos and bikes, or just cabs?
Bangalore metro boundary (~12M people)?
Account for Namma Metro cannibalization?
pain_points: Risk of conflating trips requested with trips completed
Demand is uneven across day-parts (morning vs. evening peak)
Monday-specific behavior shifts (higher corporate travel)
Metro expansion has shifted some cab demand
framework_steps: Anchor the population (Bangalore metro ~12M)
Segment by usage (commuters, occasional, tourists)
Estimate trips/day per segment
Layer supply constraints and cancellations
Sanity check against known data points

sections.heading: Approach
bullets: Break into morning peak (7-10am), daytime, evening peak (5-9pm), late night
Acknowledge Namma Metro cannibalization and Monday-specific corporate/airport travel

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: trips completed (not requested) - completion rate matters
Counter: Monday cancellation rates run ~20-25%

title: Estimate the number of grocery orders placed on Blinkit in Delhi-NCR on a Sunday morning (6am-12pm)
clarifying_qs: Define the Sunday morning window (6am-12pm)?
What's the Delhi-NCR boundary (~30M people)?
Should we work in households (~8M) rather than individuals?
What Blinkit penetration to assume (~12%)?
What order frequency per active household?
pain_points: Must convert population to households then apply penetration
Sunday-morning peak multiplier is uncertain
Slot unavailability spikes on Sunday mornings
Need to validate the final number with a sanity check
framework_steps: Clarify window and boundary
Segment population into households (~8M)
Estimate active Blinkit households (~12% -> ~1M)
Apply order frequency (~1.5/week) and Sunday share (~20%)
Apply morning peak multiplier and sanity-check

sections.heading: Calculation
bullets: 8M households -> ~1M active (12% penetration)
1M x 1.5 orders/week x 0.20 Sunday share = ~300K; morning slot -> ~80-100K orders
Sanity check: ~1 in 300 Delhi residents in a 6-hour window feels right

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: orders per active user per week (frequency metric)
State assumptions explicitly and validate the landed number

title: Estimate the number of Uber rides taken in Mumbai on a weekday morning (7-10 AM)
clarifying_qs: Uber only (not Ola/autos)?
Mumbai metro, weekday, 7-10 AM window?
Use working population, not total city population?
What funnel: commute by road > cab users > Uber share?
Should we cross-check against the supply side?
pain_points: Using total population instead of working population overstates demand
Need a realistic funnel through road commute, cab use, and Uber share
Ambiguity in the 3-hour window fraction
Must triangulate demand estimate with supply
framework_steps: Clarify (Uber only, Mumbai metro, 7-10 AM)
Segment demand (commuters, airport, school, outskirts)
Build the funnel from working population to rides in window
Cross-check with active drivers x rides/hour
State assumptions and adjust

sections.heading: Approach
bullets: Base on ~6M Mumbai office workers, not total population
Funnel: % road commute > % cab users > % choosing Uber over Ola > 3-hr window fraction

sections.heading: Answer & Cross-check
bullets: Estimated range: 250,000-350,000 rides in the 7-10 AM window
Supply check: ~80,000 active drivers x 3-4 rides/hr peak ~= 270,000

title: Estimate the number of restaurants in Bengaluru, India
clarifying_qs: How do we define a restaurant - sit-and-dine only, no delivery/pickup-only?
Do restaurants within hotels count? Do cafes count?
Which year do we assume (e.g. 2024)?
Entire city or specific areas?
Area-based or population-based approach?
pain_points: Area approach ignores demand and is distorted by hotspots like Koramangala/Whitefield
Restaurants aren't spread evenly across the city
Need to factor out street-food/quick-service outlets
Must sanity-check against real-world data (~12-15K sit-and-dine)
framework_steps: Clarify the definition (sit-and-dine, include cafes, exclude hotels)
Choose an approach (population-driven is better)
Estimate restaurants per density block or per N people
Aggregate to a total
Sanity-check against known data

sections.heading: Population Approach (preferred)
bullets: ~12M people; 1 sit-and-dine restaurant per ~200 people -> 60,000 outlets
Factor out ~75% street-food/QSR -> ~15,000 sit-and-dine restaurants
Sanity check: real-world data confirms ~12-15K

sections.heading: Area Approach (and why it fails)
bullets: Divide city into high/medium/low density blocks and estimate restaurants/sq km
Ignores demand; hotspots distort density - landed off the sane range

title: North Star for an 'AI Employee' product like Ema
clarifying_qs: Single North Star for the company or per product line (recruiting, support, FinOps)?
Who do we optimize for - buyer (CXO), user (function head), or operator (admin)?
Pre-PMF or post-PMF?
Is pricing seat-based, task-based, or platform-based?
What's the time horizon - next 12 months or longer?
user_segments: User (function head) who relies on outputs
pain_points: DAU is anti-thesis - AI should reduce human time, not require it
Vanity metrics (agents created, tasks executed) don't capture value
Time-saved alone is hard to attribute; ARR is a trailing indicator
Any metric can be gamed without quality + durability checks
framework_steps: Clarify scope and stakeholder
Reject bad North Stars and explain why
Define what 'value delivered' means (work happened, correct, trusted)
Recommend the North Star and decompose it
Add supporting, counter, leading, and activation metrics
Show why it's defensible against gaming

sections.heading: The North Star
bullets: Net Successful Autonomous Completions per customer per week
Tasks Ema completed end-to-end, auto-approved or requiring no significant correction within 7 days
Captures volume, quality, and durability; normalized per customer and per week

sections.heading: Decomposition (each a product lever)
bullets: Net Successful Completions = Total Tasks x Autonomous Rate x Success Rate x (1 - Correction Rate)
Maps to four owner teams: Growth, Trust/Autonomy, Model/Workflow Quality, Calibration

sections.heading: Counter-metrics (non-negotiable)
bullets: Hallucination rate, customer-reported error rate, cost per task, escalation rate, false-autonomy rate

sections.heading: Activation & Gaming Defense
bullets: Activation: % of new customers completing 50+ successful autonomous tasks in first 30 days
7-day correction window punishes volume/autonomy/quality gaming; per-customer normalization stops customer gaming

title: Design the 'trust' layer for enterprise AI output (Ema)
clarifying_qs: Specific workflow (finance, recruiting) or universal across all outputs?
Whose trust - power users, skeptical reviewers, or executives?
High-stakes outputs (offer letters, refunds) or low-stakes (drafts, summaries)?
What's the current trust gap - too much friction, not enough verification, or none?
Net-new design or improving existing trust UX?
user_segments: Skeptical reviewers in high-stakes functions (finance, compliance, hiring)
Power users who use Ema daily
pain_points: 'I don't know where this came from' - no provenance or citations
'I don't know how confident Ema is' - output looks identical at 99% or 51%
Verifying takes as long as doing it myself - trust UX adds friction
One bad output causes asymmetric trust damage; errors of omission are invisible
Corrections don't seem to change future behavior
framework_steps: Clarify scope and pick the segment/use case (skeptical reviewer, high-stakes)
State the JTBD (verify correctness quickly)
Rank pain points by severity x frequency
Design the trust components
Design the core flow (fast for high-confidence, deep for low)
Activation plan, metrics, risks

sections.heading: Trust Components
bullets: Provenance and citations - click any claim to see its source
Calibrated confidence signaling (high/medium/low badges, not raw %)
Collapsed reasoning trace; explicit 'what Ema didn't use' (errors of omission)
Verification suggestions, in-context past performance, one-click correction with learning loop

sections.heading: Core Flow
bullets: Output with highlighted citations, confidence badge, 'show reasoning', a 'Verify' panel, Approve/Correct/Reject, past-performance footer
Fast for high-confidence (one glance, one click), deep for low-confidence (expanded by default)

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: % of outputs approved without modification; Supporting: time to approval, % expanding reasoning/verify
Counter: false-trust rate (approved-then-wrong), confidence calibration error, tenant NPS

sections.heading: Risks & Insight
bullets: Risks: citation fatigue, confidence gaming, reviewer offloading
Insight: match verification effort to risk - high-confidence+low-stakes = one glance, low-confidence+high-stakes = full expand

title: Design human-in-the-loop: when Ema acts autonomously vs. asks
clarifying_qs: What action categories - drafts (low), communications (medium), financial/hiring (high)?
Single global setting or per-workflow/per-action configurable?
Greenfield design or improving existing autonomy logic?
Failure mode being solved - too many interruptions or too many bad autonomous actions?
Single user or multi-user enterprise with approval chains?
user_segments: New users who need more confirmations by default
pain_points: Too many interruptions make users disable autonomy entirely
Too many autonomous actions backfire on irreversible/high-stakes decisions
Auto-approve fatigue defeats the safeguard
Ema being wrong but confident is the dangerous case
framework: Action x Risk x Confidence x Precedent
framework_steps: Clarify action categories and configurability
State the JTBD (act when safe, ask when not)
Apply the four variables (action, risk, confidence, precedent)
Use the decision matrix for default behavior
Layer a progressive trust model
Design UX, activation, metrics, risks

sections.heading: Decision Matrix
bullets: Drafts/summaries (reversible, low): autonomous, user reviews when ready
Schedule/routine reply (reversible w/ effort, high confidence): act then notify, allow undo
External message (hard to undo): confirm first time, then autonomous for similar
Hire/refund/financial (irreversible, high): always require explicit approval

sections.heading: Progressive Trust Model
bullets: New user: first 50 actions are explicit approvals
Build precedent: after 10 approvals of a pattern, offer 'handle automatically next time?'
User-controlled settings; reset autonomy on a regression/mistake

sections.heading: UX
bullets: Autonomous: post-action card with one-click undo within 5 minutes
Approval requests inline (not modal), pre-filled with recommendation, countdown for time-sensitive, bulk approval

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: % of actions completed autonomously; Supporting: approval rate when Ema asks, modification rate
Counter: % of autonomous actions later reversed (false-autonomy), approval timeout rate, calibration error

title: Design a feedback mechanism that improves Ema from user corrections
clarifying_qs: Short-term UX goal or long-term model/prompt improvement?
Per-tenant learning or global learning across customers?
Is the corrected output visible to other users in the tenant or private?
Privacy/compliance constraints - can corrections train shared models?
Greenfield or improving an existing feedback loop?
user_segments: Users who give explicit thumbs-down feedback
pain_points: Feedback is noisy, slow, and inconsistent (one user's 'wrong' is another's 'style')
Models can't be fine-tuned in real time without catastrophic forgetting
Bad feedback compounds into degraded behavior
Cross-enterprise feedback can't be naively mixed (compliance, competitive)
Users stop giving feedback if they don't see results
framework_steps: Clarify goal, scope (per-tenant vs global), privacy
Capture three feedback types (implicit, modification, explicit)
Run the pipeline: capture -> categorize -> aggregate -> validate vs evals -> deploy -> close the loop
Use a two-track (per-tenant + global) architecture
Design UX, activation, metrics, risks

sections.heading: Three Feedback Types
bullets: Implicit (accepted/used downstream) - silent positive, captured automatically
Modification (the diff) - what was wrong, what was the fix; lightly categorized
Explicit (thumbs-down/flag) - optional structured tag and free-text

sections.heading: Pipeline
bullets: Capture in the natural flow -> categorize -> aggregate patterns across users/time
Validate against evals BEFORE any change ships (the step most products skip)
Deploy versioned/rollback-able; close the loop so users see feedback mattered

sections.heading: Two-Track Architecture
bullets: Per-tenant: enterprise terminology/policy, compliance-safe, visibly learns
Global: cross-enterprise patterns, gated by full regression suites, slower and careful

sections.heading: Metrics & Insight
bullets: Primary: repeat-correction rate (should fall); Supporting: feedback capture rate, eval pass rate, time-to-applied-change
Counter: regression rate, tenant-isolation breaches, overfitting to a loud recent sample
Insight: a feedback mechanism that doesn't feed evals is theater

title: You're PM at a startup building an AI-powered legal document reviewer for Indian SMBs. Define your North Star metric and defend it.
clarifying_qs: What core transformation does the product deliver - time saved, risk avoided, cost reduced?
What's the user journey - upload doc > AI flags risks > user acts > outcome?
When does the user feel the 'aha moment'?
Should the NSM capture both breadth and depth of value?
Can the metric be gamed, and does it correlate with revenue/retention?
user_segments: First-time users testing on a real document
Repeat users relying on AI flags
pain_points: 'Docs reviewed' is an output, not an outcome
Users may act on bad recommendations
Hallucination risk undermines trust
High override rate signals broken model trust
framework_steps: Define the core value (transformation delivered)
Map the user journey to the aha moment
Propose an NSM capturing breadth + depth of value
Stress-test it for gaming and correlation with revenue/retention
Add AI-specific health counter-metrics

sections.heading: Strong Answer Hints
bullets: Don't pick 'docs reviewed' - that's an output, not an outcome
Strong NSM: # of documents where the user acted on at least one AI recommendation per week
Captures engagement + trust + utility

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary NSM: weekly active documents with >=1 actioned AI recommendation
Counter-metric: AI recommendation override rate; if >40%, model trust is broken

sections.heading: Activation Angle
bullets: Onboard with a free first review on a real uploaded contract - nothing builds trust faster than catching a clause they missed

title: You're a PM at a startup building an AI-powered sales email assistant. Define your North Star metric and how you'd prevent gaming it.
clarifying_qs: Is the user an SDR/AE, and does the AI draft/personalise outbound sequences?
Is success defined by usage or by outcome quality (deals closed)?
Should the NSM capture value delivered, not just AI output generated?
How could reps game the metric?
How do we instrument it end-to-end?
user_segments: Reps at risk of abandoning AI if quality is poor
pain_points: Vanity NSMs like 'emails sent with AI assist' measure adoption, not value
Reps may force AI assist on every email to inflate numbers
Bad AI emails cause reps to stop using the tool
Spam/unsubscribe risk from AI-generated emails
framework_steps: Clarify the product and user
Define success as outcome quality, not usage
Propose an NSM tied to pipeline
Add counter-metrics to keep it honest
State end-to-end measurement

sections.heading: Strong Answer Hints
bullets: Avoid 'emails sent with AI assist' - measures adoption, not value
Strong NSM: meetings booked per AI-assisted sequence - ties AI output to pipeline
Track AI draft acceptance rate as a leading health metric

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary (NSM): meetings booked per AI-assisted email sequence vs. non-AI baseline
Counter-metric: unsubscribe rate and spam complaints on AI-generated emails

sections.heading: Activation Angle
bullets: Onboard reps by generating a personalised sample email in under 30 seconds from CRM data - first value before they send

title: You're a PM at a B2B SaaS startup building an AI email assistant for sales teams. What's your North Star metric and how do you measure AI quality?
clarifying_qs: Does the AI draft/personalise outbound emails for SDRs and AEs?
Is the core value time saved, improved reply rates, or both?
Which NSM candidate - emails sent (vanity), reply rate (outcome), or hours saved (efficiency)?
What separate AI-quality metrics should we track?
What's the hallucination/trust guardrail?
user_segments: Users who heavily edit AI drafts
pain_points: 'Emails sent' is a vanity metric reflecting AI activity, not value
Product health and AI quality metrics get conflated
AI inventing a prospect detail is a trust-killer
Users rewriting 80%+ of a draft means the AI adds no value
framework: AI NSM + Product Health vs. AI Quality
framework_steps: Define the product (AI drafts/personalises outbound)
Identify the core value (time saved AND reply-rate lift)
Compare NSM candidates and pick/defend one
Separate AI-quality metrics (acceptance, edit distance, regeneration)
Add hallucination/fact-accuracy guardrails

sections.heading: Strong Answer Hints
bullets: NSM should reflect user success, not AI activity
Separate product-health metrics (activation, retention) from AI-quality metrics (acceptance rate, edit distance)
One AI-invented prospect detail can kill adoption - add a fact-accuracy guardrail

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: reply rate on AI-generated emails vs. manual baseline (aim for parity or +10%)
Counter-metric: edit distance - track 'high-edit sessions' (>80% rewritten) as a quality failure

sections.heading: Activation Angle
bullets: First-value: user gets a first AI-drafted email accepted (<30% edits) within session 1 - instrument as the activation event

title: Customer-support agent resolution dropped 70% -> 55% over two weeks
clarifying_qs: Is the drop confirmed in data or based on one complaint? Anecdote vs. signal.
Sudden cliff or gradual decline?
One customer, a segment, or all customers?
Did we ship anything - prompt, model swap, KB update, integration change?
Did the model provider push a silent update? Did the 'resolved' definition change?
user_segments: One enterprise vs. one industry vs. all customers
Ticket types / intent classes degrading differently
pain_points: Resolution rate isn't atomic - a small drop in one step compounds end-to-end
Hosted LLMs change silently
Customer-side KB/policy changes go unnotified
Tracking/definition changes can fake a drop
framework_steps: Open with clarifying questions (signal vs. anecdote, cliff vs. gradual, scope, recent changes)
Decompose the metric into its chain
Scope the drop (customer, intent, channel, time)
Generate hypotheses in three buckets (internal, external, data/tracking)
Prioritize by likelihood x ease-to-check, then validate
Recommend, define success

sections.heading: Decompose the Metric
bullets: Resolution rate = Intent classification x Retrieval x Generation quality x Action execution x User acceptance
Find the moved term first before hypothesizing

sections.heading: Hypothesis Buckets
bullets: Internal/product: silent model regression, prompt/KB regression, stale vector index, cost-saving model swap, broken integration
External/customer: new product creating new ticket types, customer KB changes, seasonal spikes
Data/tracking: 'resolution' definition changed, logging gaps, sampling bias

sections.heading: Prioritize & Validate
bullets: Highest: silent provider update and our-own prompt/KB regression (high likelihood, easy to check)
Re-run the production eval suite; pull 50 failed cases and trace each to find the modal failure mode

sections.heading: Recommend & Success
bullets: Pattern A (silent model regression): lock to previous model, re-run evals, build a regression suite for every provider update
Pattern B (KB drift): refresh/re-index, add continuous KB-freshness checks + alerting
Success: resolution >=68% within 7 days, no eval regression, false-resolution rate doesn't rise

title: Recruiting candidate quality dropped after a model update
clarifying_qs: Model swap (different provider) or a version update (e.g. Claude 3.7 -> 4)?
How is 'candidate quality' defined - ranking accuracy, recruiter accept rate, interview rate, time-to-hire?
Did our eval suite catch this before deployment?
Is the drop uniform or concentrated in certain roles/geographies?
Did anything else change besides the model - prompts, embeddings, scoring weights?
user_segments: Different roles (tech vs. ops, senior vs. junior, US vs. India)
pain_points: A new model can parse, score, and calibrate differently
Few-shot examples tuned for the old model may not generalize
Bias profiles can shift along seniority/industry/geography
Embeddings swap can break retrieval upstream; thresholds may not carry over
framework_steps: Clarify swap vs. version update and the quality definition
Decompose where the model swap could hurt (JD parse, candidate parse, matching, ranking, explanation)
Generate hypotheses (model-behavior, prompt-model mismatch, system integration)
Validate via historical evals + side-by-side old/new traces + A/B holdout
Recommend rollback + workflow-pinned models (EmaFusion routing)

sections.heading: Where the Swap Could Hurt
bullets: JD parsing, candidate parsing, semantic matching, ranking/scoring, explanation generation
New models parse fields differently, grade with different strictness, and calibrate confidence differently

sections.heading: Hypothesis Buckets
bullets: Model-behavior: stricter grading, tokenization parse issues, non-generalizing few-shots, shifted bias
Prompt-model mismatch: prompts tuned for the old model interpreted differently
System integration: embeddings also swapped, scoring thresholds didn't carry over

sections.heading: Validate & Recommend
bullets: Run new model on historical evals; trace 50 flagged candidates old vs. new; 20% A/B holdout on old model
Immediate: roll back, communicate forensics, pause auto model-rollout
Strategic (EmaFusion): pin each workflow to its best-scoring model; gate updates on workflow-specific evals

sections.heading: Counter-metrics
bullets: Recruiter accept rate of top-K, time-to-hire, adverse-impact ratio across demographics, explanation hallucination rate

title: Hallucination complaints spiked from one client, nothing changed in the product
clarifying_qs: Confirm 'nothing changed' - no deploys, prompt changes, or model updates (including silent provider ones)?
Did complaint volume rise or did the hallucination rate per query rise?
Absolute count or proportion?
Did the type of hallucination change (factual, fabricated entities, wrong tool calls)?
Exact week/day it started, and is the client's usage pattern the same?
user_segments: New user cohort onboarded at the client
pain_points: If the product didn't change, the cause is outside the product
Client KB drift creates ambiguous retrieval the model resolves by fabricating
Silent model-provider updates shift calibration
New users/queries surface edge cases the system never trained on
framework_steps: Confirm nothing changed internally; clarify rate vs. volume and type
Enumerate external changes (input data, usage pattern, upstream model, detection/labeling, environment)
Diagnose with 20 concrete examples traced end-to-end and categorized
Find the modal cause; check KB ingestion logs, provider changelog, usage logs
Recommend per pattern; validate with rate-based counter-metrics

sections.heading: What Changed in the World
bullets: Input data: client added conflicting/stale KB docs; format/template changes degrade parsing
Usage: new user cohort, matured power users, rising query complexity
Upstream model: silent provider update, rate-limit fallback to a smaller model, changed guardrails
Detection: new reviewer, widened 'hallucination' definition, new internal audit

sections.heading: Diagnosis
bullets: Pull 20 recent examples; trace query -> retrieved context -> output -> action
Categorize by failure mode; often 70%+ share one root cause
Check KB ingestion logs (last 4-6 weeks), provider changelog, usage logs

sections.heading: Recommend by Pattern
bullets: A - KB drift: audit/dedupe/version docs, retrain retrieval
B - silent model update: lock the version, re-evaluate; multi-model routing mitigates
C - new query types: add adversarial evals, harden prompts, add explicit 'I don't know'

sections.heading: Counter-metrics
bullets: Hallucination rate per 1000 queries (not absolute count), customer-reported vs. internally-detected gap, calibration, tenant health

title: Average order value on your e-commerce platform drops 18% week-over-week. How do you diagnose and address it?
clarifying_qs: Is the drop global or segment-specific (category, geo, device, new vs. returning)?
Could it be a data/instrumentation issue (tagging change, attribution shift)?
Did orders go up while revenue stayed flat, or revenue drop with flat orders?
Did anything change this week (pricing experiment, coupon campaign, feed ranking)?
What's the business impact to establish urgency?
user_segments: Categories with different AOV norms (electronics vs. fashion vs. grocery)
pain_points: Jumping to solutions before exhausting diagnosis
Mix shift to lower-priced items goes undetected without category segmentation
Promo/discount spikes or feed-ranking changes silently move AOV
Supply issues in high-value categories
framework_steps: Scope the drop (global vs. segment)
Rule out data/instrumentation issues
Break down AOV = revenue / orders
Hypothesize causes (mix shift, promo spike, supply, algo change)
Propose a 48-hour diagnostic, then a solution by root cause

sections.heading: Strong Answer Hints
bullets: Don't jump to solutions - exhaust diagnosis first
Sharpest move: segment AOV by category first (wildly different AOV norms)
Quantify impact early: 18% AOV drop x 1M orders/week = significant revenue at risk

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: AOV segmented by category, acquisition channel, and user cohort
Counter-metric: order volume - if AOV drops but orders surge, net revenue may still be up

sections.heading: Activation Angle
bullets: If root cause is mix shift to lower-priced items: test 'frequently bought together' bundles and a free-delivery minimum-order threshold

title: How would you measure the success of a new 'Buy Again' shortcut feature added to Zepto's home screen?
clarifying_qs: What exactly does the feature do (surface past orders, tap to re-add to cart)?
What does 'working' mean - engagement, incremental orders, or retention lift?
What funnel stages should we measure?
Does it increase order frequency for returning users?
Could it cannibalize discovery/search or shrink basket size?
user_segments: Users who reorder a small set of staples
pain_points: Engagement metrics (CTR) get confused with impact metrics
Feature must create incremental orders, not just relocate existing intent
Cannibalizing catalog exploration could hurt LTV
Basket may shrink if users only reorder one item
framework_steps: Clarify the feature
Define what success means
Map funnel metrics (impression -> click -> add-to-cart -> checkout -> order)
Add the retention angle (order frequency lift)
Set guardrails and design an A/B test with holdout

sections.heading: Strong Answer Hints
bullets: Show funnel logic: the feature only succeeds if it creates incremental orders
Distinguish engagement (CTR) from impact (incremental order rate, repeat-frequency lift)
Call out cannibalization: reduced exploration can hurt LTV even while boosting short-term conversion

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: incremental orders/week attributed to 'Buy Again' (A/B treatment vs. control)
Counter-metric: avg items per order - a shrinking basket is a revenue problem

sections.heading: Activation Angle
bullets: Activates when a returning user (2+ prior orders) taps 'Buy Again' and checks out; track 7-day repeat rate vs. non-users

title: How would you measure the success of Meesho's newly launched in-app video shopping feature?
clarifying_qs: Is the goal discovery, conversion, engagement, or all three?
What's the funnel - impressions > play > watch time > product tap > add to cart > purchase?
How should we layer metrics by stage?
How do we segment (new vs. returning, Tier 1 vs. Tier 2/3, category)?
What guardrails tell us the feature is hurting the core experience?
user_segments: High-intent search users vs. home-feed browsers
pain_points: Listing metrics without ranking them
Price-sensitive Tier 2/3 base may gain trust before conversion (a lag effect)
Video could increase scroll depth but kill conversion
Risk of hurting the core shopping experience
framework_steps: Clarify the goal (discovery, conversion, engagement)
Define the video funnel
Layer metrics by stage (awareness, engagement, conversion, retention)
Segment the analysis
Set guardrails and propose a holdout experiment

sections.heading: Strong Answer Hints
bullets: Rank metrics: primary = GMV influenced by video; secondary = engagement depth
Acknowledge Tier 2/3 trust-before-conversion lag
Propose a holdout: control sees no video vs. test, compare 30-day GMV and retention

sections.heading: Metrics
bullets: Primary: GMV attributed to video-assisted sessions (>10s watched before purchase)
Counter-metric: session abandonment for video-served vs. not - if scroll depth rises but conversion falls, it's net negative

sections.heading: Activation Angle
bullets: Surface video thumbnails in search results first (not home feed) - high-intent users give cleaner signal fast
